Question: A previously healthy man in his 20s who was of Asian heritage presented with a nontender mass in the left parotid region, which had been slowly enlarging for 18 months. He had experienced occasional blepharospasm and paresthesia in the ipsilateral eye and cheek. He had also noted an unintended 3-kg weight loss in the same duration, which he subjectively attributed to the stress of school. Physical examination demonstrated a firm, immobile, irregular mass n the left parotid and approximately 3 3 cm in size. Mild left-sided buccal weakness was noted. Otherwise, his cranial nerve function was intact. Fine needle aspiration demonstrated medium-size ovoid cells with minimal cytoplasm in crowded groups. T2-weighted magnet resonance imaging following the initial computed tomographic (CT) scan demonstrated a poorly marginated mass that was 4 cm in maximum diameter abutting the left superficial parotid with involvement of the masseter (Figure, A). A parotidectomy with excision of the tumor was planned. During the initial exposure of the tumor, an intraoperative frozen section demonstrated a cellular infiltrate, and a diagnosis of lymphoma could not be excluded. Given the size and fixed nature of the tumor, the decision was made to stop resection on establishing a definitive diagnosis. Flow cytometry demonstrated phenotypically normal T-cell and B-cell populations. Permanent histologic sections demonstrated nests of nonkeratinizing undifferentiated atypical epithelial cells intimately intermingled with a dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate (Figure, B). Immunohistochemical analysis for cytokeratin AE1/3 was positive in the carcinoma cells (Figure, C). In situ hybridization for Epstein-Barr virus encoded small RNAs (EBERs) was positive (Figure, D).A, T2-weighted coronal magnetic resonance image of the face. B-D, Histopathologic images. B, Hematoxylin-eosin, original magnification  20. C, Cytokeratin AE1/3 immunostain, original magnification  20. D, Epstein-Barr virus in situ hybridization, original magnification  20. What Is Your Diagnosis?
Options: A:Lymphoepithelial carcinoma of parotid gland B:Undifferentiated carcinoma of parotid gland C:Lymphoma D:Metastatic squamous carcinoma to parotid gland
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
opd: Metastatic squamous carcinoma to parotid gland
diagnosis: A. Lymphoepithelial carcinoma of parotid gland
explanation: The differential diagnosis of a parotid mass consists of a long list of benign and malignant conditions. Malignant lesions in the parotid gland commonly include mucoepidermoid carcinoma, adenoid cystic carcinoma, and other adenocarcinomas.1 Lymphoepithelial carcinoma (LEC) is one of the rarest primary malignant neoplasms of the parotid gland, representing less than 1% of all parotid tumors in the United States.1,2 A review of the Western literature for parotid LEC from 1975 to 2014 was performed with MEDLINE. Case reports and case series were examined and revealed a total of 78 cases. The median age at presentation reported was at the mid-fifth decade of life (range, 19-70 years). The pathological feature of LEC is defined by poorly differentiated, large, atypical epithelial cells, with benign inflammatory infiltrates composed of lymphocytes and plasma cells.1,2 It can arise from multiple locations in the head and neck region,1 including the nasopharynx, larynx, tonsils, and salivary glands. Among the salivary glands, the parotid gland is most commonly associated with this carcinoma (65%).2,3Lymphoepithelial carcinoma has a strong genetic predilection. It has approximately a 10-fold increase in incidence in the Southern Chinese, Eskimo, and Inuit populations in Alaska, Greenland, and northern Canada.3-5 These populations are predisposed to acquiring LEC in the nasopharynx and salivary glands.4,5 Among the published cases, 84% to 87.5% of parotid LEC cases had evidence of Epstein-Barr virus positivity,5 suggesting a possible environmental or infectious risk. Patients with parotid LEC commonly present with a painless or mildly tender, slowly growing mass in the preauricular region. Facial nerve paralysis is seldom observed.4 Owing to the insidious onset, the rate of nodal metastasis at the time of presentation is reported to be as high as 40%,3-5 which is particularly common in non-Asian populations. Distant metastasis is rarely reported.2-5Presurgical diagnosis of LEC has not been well illustrated in the literature. A few CT findings seen commonly in malignant neoplasms of the parotid, such as contrast enhancement, poorly defined margins, the lack of cystic degeneration or calcification, are also seen in cases of LEC, but these features are neither consistent nor specific for this disease.6 Magnetic resonance imaging also does not allow for discrimination of LEC from other malignant neoplasms.6 Fine needle aspiration of LEC can demonstrate cytologic features such as cohesive neoplastic clusters, pleomorphic nuclei, condensed chromatin, and eosinophilic nucleoli.7 Unfortunately, these characteristics may not be distinguishable from reactive lymphohistiocytic cells, lymphoma, or undifferentiated carcinoma.7 Therefore, the diagnosis is dependent on pathological examination of the tissue.Owing to the rare nature of this disease, there has not been a consensus on the treatment for LEC of the salivary gland. Most published case reports or case series have included tumor excision accompanied by varying degrees of gland excision, with or without modified radical neck dissection and adjuvant radiation therapy.8,9 Similar outcomes are achieved by using definitive chemoradiation in cases of LEC at locations that are difficult to treat surgically. The overall 5-year survival with treatment is about 60% to 70%.8,9 Although treatment options have not been standardized, it is recommended to rule out possible nasopharyngeal carcinoma as the primary site of the malignant neoplasm before definitive treatment.10 PET/CT is commonly used in this scenario to examine if other areas, including nasopharynx, are involved.9,10 Nasoendoscopy and biopsy following PET/CT are indicated to rule out microscopic disease at alternative primary site of LEC. In this case, the patient underwent PET/CT and random biopsies of the nasopharynx following initial surgery. No evidence of malignant disease at sites other than the parotid gland was found.
